subject: math
title: Grade 3 Math — Go Math Texas Assessment Guide

standards.subject: math
standards.description: Represent fractions with models / equal parts

standards.subject: math
standards.description: Linear measurement (length to nearest unit) — prerequisite

prompt: What is another way to write 20 + 6?
explanation: 20 + 6 in standard form is 26.

prompt: Use the numbers 1,075, 950, and 876. Which number is greater than 1,000?
explanation: Only 1,075 has a 1 in the thousands place, so it is greater than 1,000.

prompt: Use the numbers 1,075, 950, and 876. Which number is less than 900?
explanation: 876 has 8 hundreds, fewer than 900; 950 and 1,075 are both greater.

prompt: What number names the point on the number line?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 380 80" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="18" y1="40" x2="362" y2="40" stroke="black" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)" marker-start="url(#b)"/><defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6" fill="black"/></marker><marker id="b" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="0" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M6,0 L0,3 L6,6" fill="black"/></marker></defs><line x1="30.0" y1="33" x2="30.0" y2="47" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30.0" y="64" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">170</text><line x1="94.0" y1="33" x2="94.0" y2="47" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="94.0" y="64" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">180</text><line x1="158.0" y1="33" x2="158.0" y2="47" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="158.0" y="64" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">190</text><line x1="222.0" y1="33" x2="222.0" y2="47" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="222.0" y="64" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">200</text><line x1="286.0" y1="33" x2="286.0" y2="47" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="286.0" y="64" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">210</text><line x1="350.0" y1="33" x2="350.0" y2="47" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="350.0" y="64" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">220</text><circle cx="222.0" cy="40" r="6" fill="black"/></svg>

explanation: The ticks count by tens: 170, 180, 190, 200, 210, 220. The dot is on the 4th tick after 170 — 200.
workedSolution: Count by tens from 170; the point is at 200.

prompt: Use the numbers 759, 1,043, and 1,069. Which number makes the comparison true?  1,183 > 986 > ___
explanation: The unknown must be less than 986. Only 759 is less than 986.

prompt: Use the numbers 759, 1,043, and 1,069. Which could be the unknown number?  784 < 1,052 < ___
explanation: The unknown must be greater than 1,052. 1,043 < 1,052, but 1,069 > 1,052.

prompt: Carmen has 3 same-size pizzas. The cheese pizza is cut into 6 equal slices, the mushroom into 4 equal slices, and the sausage into 12 equal slices. Which pizza has the largest slices?
explanation: The fewer equal pieces a whole is cut into, the larger each piece. 4 slices is the fewest, so the mushroom pizza has the largest slices.
workedSolution: Fewest pieces (4) = largest slices → mushroom pizza.

prompt: What is a name for the equal parts in the shape?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 90" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10.0" y="10" width="90.0" height="70" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="100.0" y="10" width="90.0" height="70" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

explanation: The square is divided into 2 equal parts, which are called halves.

prompt: Bristol has 2 loaves of bread that are the same size. She cuts each loaf into 4 equal pieces and uses 6 pieces. How much bread did Bristol use?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 230 100" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10.0" y="10" width="52.5" height="34" fill="#f4c542" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="62.5" y="10" width="52.5" height="34" fill="#f4c542" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="115.0" y="10" width="52.5" height="34" fill="#f4c542" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="167.5" y="10" width="52.5" height="34" fill="#f4c542" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="10.0" y="56" width="52.5" height="34" fill="#f4c542" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="62.5" y="56" width="52.5" height="34" fill="#f4c542" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="115.0" y="56" width="52.5" height="34" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="167.5" y="56" width="52.5" height="34" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
figures.alt: two bars each in 4 parts, 6 parts shaded
figures.caption: Each loaf is cut into 4 equal pieces.

explanation: Each piece is 1/4 of a loaf. She used 6 pieces, so 6 × 1/4 = 6/4 (which is 1 1/2 loaves).

prompt: What number goes in the box?   26 = 20 + 6,   38 = 30 + 8,   so 26 + 38 = 50 + 14 = ___
explanation: Add by place value: 20 + 30 = 50 and 6 + 8 = 14, then 50 + 14 = 64.

prompt: Which shape shows thirds?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><text x="50" y="18" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle">A</text><circle cx="50" cy="60" r="38" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="18" y1="88" x2="86" y2="40" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="12" y1="66" x2="34" y2="96" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="18" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle">B</text><circle cx="150" cy="60" r="38" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="150" y1="60" x2="150.0" y2="22.0" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="150" y1="60" x2="117.1" y2="79.0" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="150" y1="60" x2="182.9" y2="79.0" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="250" y="18" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle">C</text><circle cx="250" cy="60" r="38" fill="white" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="250" y1="60" x2="269.0" y2="27.1" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="250" y1="60" x2="214.3" y2="73.0" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="250" y1="60" x2="269.0" y2="92.9" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
figures.alt: three circles A B C; B shows 3 equal parts

options.rationale: Shape A is split by chords into unequal parts, not 3 equal parts.
Shape C is split into 3 parts that are not equal.

explanation: Thirds means 3 equal parts. Circle B is divided into 3 equal parts.

prompt: Jess saw 7 deer in the morning. In the afternoon, Jess saw double that number. How many deer did Jess see in the afternoon?
explanation: Double means 2 times as many: 2 × 7 = 14.

prompt: What tens fact can you use to find 15 − 7?
explanation: Make a ten: subtract 5 from 15 to reach the ten (15 − 5 = 10), then subtract 2 more to get 8.

prompt: Pam has 44 bows. She makes 17 more bows and Betty gives her 35 bows. How many bows does Pam have now?
explanation: Add all three amounts: 44 + 17 + 35 = 96.

prompt: On Saturday, Kevin slept 9 hours. There are 24 hours in a day. How many hours was Kevin awake on Saturday?
explanation: Subtract sleeping hours from the whole day: 24 − 9 = 15.

prompt: Mr. Brown has a box of 75 markers. He gives 18 markers to his students. How many markers does Mr. Brown have now?
explanation: Subtract the markers given away: 75 − 18 = 57.

prompt: There are 46 students on a playground. Of these, 28 are girls and the rest are boys. What number sentence can you use to find how many of the students are boys?
explanation: The boys are the total minus the girls: 46 − 28 = 18.

prompt: There are 245 hardcover books and 550 paperback books in a bookstore. More paperback books are delivered. Now there are 995 books. How many books were delivered?
explanation: First find the books already there: 245 + 550 = 795. Then 995 − 795 = 200 delivered.

prompt: There are 276 people at a dog show. Then 472 more people come. What number sentence can you use to find the number of people at the dog show now?
explanation: Join the two groups by adding: 276 + 472 = 748.

prompt: There are 322 fans at the soccer field on Wednesday and 228 fans on Thursday. What number sentence can you use to find the number of fans on both days?
explanation: Add the two days together: 322 + 228 = 550.

prompt: Amy buys a bag of popcorn with these coins and receives no change. How much does the popcorn cost?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 316 90" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="42" cy="40" r="26" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="42" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">25¢</text><circle cx="110" cy="40" r="26" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="110" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">25¢</text><circle cx="178" cy="40" r="26" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="178" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">25¢</text><circle cx="242" cy="40" r="22" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="242" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">5¢</text><circle cx="302" cy="40" r="22" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="302" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">5¢</text></svg>
figures.alt: coins: three quarters and two nickels

explanation: Add the coin values: 25 + 25 + 25 + 5 + 5 = 85 cents.

prompt: Isabel has these coins. What is the total value of the coins (in cents)?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 376 90" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="42" cy="40" r="26" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="42" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">25¢</text><circle cx="110" cy="40" r="26" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="110" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">25¢</text><circle cx="178" cy="40" r="26" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="178" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">25¢</text><circle cx="242" cy="40" r="22" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="242" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">5¢</text><circle cx="302" cy="40" r="22" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="302" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">5¢</text><circle cx="362" cy="40" r="22" fill="#d9c8a0" stroke="#7a6a3a" stroke-width="2"/><text x="362" y="45" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle">5¢</text></svg>
figures.alt: coins: three quarters and three nickels

explanation: Add the coin values: 25 + 25 + 25 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 90 cents.

prompt: Gary has 8 plates. He puts 3 waffles on each plate. How many waffles are on the plates?
explanation: Equal groups: 8 plates × 3 waffles = 24.

prompt: There are 14 balloons. Each child gets 2 balloons. How many children are there?
explanation: Divide into equal groups: 14 ÷ 2 = 7 children.

prompt: There are an even number of kittens and an odd number of puppies in a pet store. Write a number of kittens and a number of puppies that might be at the pet store.
explanation: Any even count works for kittens (2, 4, 6, …) and any odd count for puppies (1, 3, 5, …). For example, 4 kittens and 3 puppies.

prompt: There are 350 rubber bands in a bag. There are 100 fewer rubber bands in Mr. Stuart's desk. How many rubber bands are in Mr. Stuart's desk?
explanation: ‘100 fewer’ means subtract: 350 − 100 = 250.

prompt: Katy drew 25 hearts on a notebook. Rita drew some hearts too. They drew 39 hearts altogether. Write a number sentence with a box for the missing number, then solve: how many hearts did Rita draw?
explanation: The two amounts join to 39: 25 + □ = 39. Subtract to solve: 39 − 25 = 14.

prompt: Mr. Troy's students made 43 pictures. Some were on the wall and 24 were in folders. Write a number sentence with a box for the missing number, then solve: how many pictures were on the wall?
explanation: Wall pictures plus folder pictures make 43: □ + 24 = 43. Subtract: 43 − 24 = 19.

prompt: How many vertices does this shape have?
explanation: The shape is an octagon. An octagon has 8 vertices (corners).

prompt: A box has 6 faces, 12 edges, and 8 vertices. What could be the shape of the box?
explanation: A solid with 6 faces, 12 edges, and 8 vertices is a rectangular prism (a cube is a special rectangular prism).

prompt: How many of these shapes have exactly 6 vertices?

figures.alt: a hexagon, an octagon, a quadrilateral, and a smaller hexagon

explanation: Count vertices: hexagon (6), octagon (8), quadrilateral (4), small hexagon (6). Two shapes have exactly 6 vertices.
workedSolution: Two hexagons have 6 vertices each → 2 shapes.

prompt: Use an inch ruler. What is the length of the eraser to the nearest inch?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="20" y="20" width="120" height="30" rx="4" fill="#c9c9c9" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="20" y1="70" x2="200" y2="70" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="20" y1="70" x2="20" y2="82" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="20" y="96" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">0</text><line x1="80" y1="70" x2="80" y2="82" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="96" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">1</text><line x1="140" y1="70" x2="140" y2="82" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="140" y="96" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">2</text><line x1="200" y1="70" x2="200" y2="82" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="200" y="96" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">3</text></svg>
figures.alt: an eraser measured on an inch ruler, about 2 inches

explanation: Lined up against the inch ruler, the eraser reaches the 2-inch mark, so it is about 2 inches long.

prompt: Emily uses color tiles to make a rectangle. She uses 6 rows of tiles and 5 columns of tiles. How many tiles does Emily use?
explanation: Rows × columns gives the total tiles: 6 × 5 = 30.

prompt: Tamara has two colors of string. If the black string is 4 inches long, about how long is the gray string?

figures.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 192 80" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="12" y="18" width="168" height="12" fill="#333" stroke="black"/><text x="12" y="14" font-size="11">black</text><rect x="12" y="48" width="126" height="12" fill="#bbb" stroke="black"/><text x="12" y="44" font-size="11">gray</text></svg>
figures.alt: black string 4 inches, gray string about 3 inches

explanation: The gray string is a little shorter than the 4-inch black string — about 3 inches.
workedSolution: Gray string ≈ 3 inches (one inch shorter than the black string).
